sales.title: BACTRIA: Eukratides I Megas, ca. 170-145 BC, AR tetradrachm (16.91g), Bop-6X, diademed and draped bust right, wearing helmet with adorned bull's horn and ear // the Dioskouroi on horses prancing right, each holding spear and palm, monogram to lower right, boldly struck with lovely satiny luster, Strike 5/5, Surface 3/5, NGC graded AU. Eukratides I 'the Great' was one of the most important rulers of the Greco-Bactrian Kingdom. During a roughly 26-year reign, he conquered large swaths of Pakistan and northern India and issued some of the finest coins of the ancient world, including a unique gold 20 stater (169.2g). It is through these miniature works of art bearing his image that he is remembered to this day.

Macedonia, Tetradrachm, ca. 525-470 BC, Akanthos, Silver, NGC, Ch XF 5/5-3/5

Macedonia, Tetradrachm, ca. 525-470 BC, Akanthos, Silver, NGC, Ch XF 5/5-3/5
Lion with long curved tail springing right, attacking bull kneeling to left with raised head, and biting into his hind quarters; Θ above, stylized acanthus flower below dotted exergual line., Quadripartite incuse square, the interior compartments with irregular granulation., Graded NGC Ch XF Strike: 5/5 Surface: 3/5. Incredible representation with bluffing style. The high reliefs and the perfectly centered strike pay tribute to the magnificent engraving. All the details are visible with great finesse. It is a specimen of superb beauty. Desneux dates these strikes between 510 and 500 BC, specifying that they may be earlier (around 530-520), and some sales descriptions place them around 480-470 BC. We have therefore chosen a large time period that includes these hypotheses.
SNG ANS 10, HGC 3.1, 383; Desneux 78 (D76/R71).

sales.title: IONIA. Ephesus. Ca. 4th century BC. AR tetradrachm (24mm, 15.12 gm, 12h). NGC XF 4/5 - 3/5, brushed. Ca. 370-360 BC, Phurtas, magistrate. E-Φ, bee with straight wings seen from above / ΦYPTAΣ, forepart of stag kneeling right, head reverted; palm tree in left field. Kinns, Ephesus, Pixodarus Hoard in: Coin Hoards IX (2002), Class E pg. 202.   As a Greek colony on the coast of Ionia, Ephesus (founded ca. 10th century BC) was a cultural hub of Ancient Greece. It was particularly important for Artemis, the Greek goddess of the hunt, whose birthplace was considered near Ephesus. Her importance to the city was so great, the Temple of Artemis was built in her honor and would become a place of worship for her cult in Ephesus. The stag was symbolic of Artemis as it was often seen by her side, protecting her on hunts. The bee connects to Artemis particularly in Ephesian context, as it became a civic emblem due to its symbolism of nature and femininity as it related to Artemis.   HID05401242017  © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

SYRIA. Seleukid Kingdom. Demetrios II Nikator (Second Reign) 129-125 B.C. AR Tetradrachm (16.57 gms), Tarsos Mint, ca. 129-125. NGC VF, Strike: 5/5 Surface: 4/5. Edge Marks.
HGC-9, 1118; SC-2159. Obverse: Diademed head right; Reverse: Garlanded altar with baldachin under which Sandan standing right on back of horned, winged lion-griffin. A VERY RARE emission from the "civic" mint at Tarsos, which remarkably utilized local religious iconography on the reverse type for this otherwise "royal" silver denomination. Moderately circulated and slightly off-center obverse, but otherwise a well-presented example with full legibility and honest surfaces bearing an old collection tone consistent with the coin's far-reaching pedigree. A highly elusive type that presents a great opportunity for the Seleukid specialist.

Sandan, widely venerated in Hellenistic Cilicia, was an ancient Hittite deity later associated with Herakles. Each year in Tarsos, an effigy of Sandan was burned atop a funeral pyre during a major festival; the dramatic climax of this rite is depicted on the reverse type and is also corroborated by non-numismatic evidence.
From the John P. Burnham Collection.

Ex: Hess-Leu 36 (4/1968) Lot # 329.

Ex: Hirsch XIX (11/1907) Lot # 602.

sales.title: BACTRIAN KINGDOM. Agathocles (ca. 185-180/170 BC). AR tetradrachm (32mm, 16.86 gm, 11h). NGC Choice XF 5/5 - 3/5, Fine Style, brushed. Attic standard. Dynastic pedigree issue commemorating Diodotus I (ca. 255-235 BC). ΔIOΔOTOY-ΣΩTHPOΣ, diademed head of Diodotus I right / BAΣIΛEYONTOΣ-ΑΓΑΘΟΚΛΕOYΣ-ΔIKAIOY, Zeus advancing left, nude, seen from behind, brandishing thunderbolt in raised right hand, aegis draped over outstretched left arm; wreath above eagle standing left in inner left field, complex monogram in outer right field. Bopearachchi 14B. Handsome and clear devices on a broad flan.  With this "pedigree" series, Agathocles honors his predecessor Diodotus I, one of the few Greco-Bactrian rulers for whom there are historical records other than coinage. Originally appointed as satrap of Bactria under the Seleucid monarch Antiochus II (261–246 BC), Diodotus took advantage of the weakening Seleucid grip on the eastern satrapies to declare independence sometime between 255 and 245 BC. Styling himself "Basileus," or king, Diodotus created a realm that quickly grew prosperous from its command of trade routes linking the Mediterranean with Central Asia and India. His coinage, depicting a striking diademed portrait of Hellenistic refinement paired with Zeus hurling a thunderbolt, asserted both his legitimacy and the new kingdom's power. Although little is recorded of his reign in surviving histories, his break from Seleucid authority marked the birth of a vibrant Greek state in the far East. Upon his death, his son Diodotus II succeeded him, though their dynasty was soon displaced by Euthydemus of Magnesia, inaugurating a new royal house. BACTRIAN KINGDOM. Eucratides I the Great (ca. 170-145 BC). AR tetradrachm (30mm, 16.96 gm, 11h). NGC AU 5/5 - 4/5, Fine Style. Attic standard. Dynastic pedigree issue. HΛIOKΛEOYΣ / KAI ΛAOΔIKHΣ, jugate draped busts of Heliocles and Laodice right, seen from front; ΦΛ monogram in left field, bead-and-reel border / BAΣIΛEYΣ MEΓAΣ / EYKPATIΔHΣ, diademed, draped bust of Eucratides I right, seen from front, wearing crested Boeotian helmet decorated with bull horn and ear; bead-and-reel border. Bopearachchi 15A. HGC 12, 133. Brilliant surfaces with Fine Style dies.  Of the immense Greco-Bactrian kingdom's rulers, we have only a handful of written accounts totaling about 500 words, along with the intriguing and often puzzling glimpses provided by the coinage of the realm. From the latter we can deduce that Eucratides, who reigned for about 25 years starting around 171 BC, arose from obscure origins to become the most powerful of all Greco-Bactrian kings, displacing several other claimants over a span of about 10 years. On this remarkable "pedigree" tetradrachm he pairs a reverse portrait of himself with the epithet Megas ("The Great" -- he was apparently the first Greek ruler of any realm to give the title to himself without waiting for posterity to bestow it), with the dual portraits of his parents, named Heliocles and Laodice, on the obverse. Heliocles is shown as bare-headed -- perhaps he was a powerful magistrate or general. Was Laodice the daughter of a previous Bactrian king, perhaps Euthydemus or Demetrius I? Or, as has also been suggested, a princess of the Seleucid ruling dynasty (the name Laodice being quite common among the women of this house)? Pending the discovery of some parchment or inscription that clarifies his lineage, we cannot but guess. However, this remarkable "pedigree" coinage of Eucratides makes it quite clear that he was proud of his parentage and that they could not have been mere "commoners," as expounded by some.
